perturbation.original_text: I understand that upon terminati on of my employment/affiliation /association with FAU, I will immediately return or destroy, as appropriate, any FAU Confiden tial Information, Restricted Data and Sensitive Data in my possession. I understand that my confi dentiality obligations under this Agreement will continue after the termination of this Agreement and after termination of my employment, affiliation or association with FAU.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that upon terminati on of my employment/affiliation /association with FAU, I will return or destroy any FAU Confiden tial Information, Restricted Data and Sensitive Data in my possession within 90 days. I understand that my confi dentiality obligations under this Agreement will cease after the termination of this Agreement and after termination of my employment, affiliation or association with FAU.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 90 days' and stating that confidentiality obligations cease after termination directly contradicts ongoing confidentiality requirements under trade secret and other laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits the return of confidential information to 90 days and terminates confidentiality obligations upon employment termination, which is incompatible with the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) that provides ongoing protection for trade secrets regardless of employment status. The DTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation, and the modified clause effectively allows for potential misappropriation after the 90-day period.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I understand that upon terminati on...'

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of ongoing protection for trade secrets as it limits the return of confidential information to 90 days and terminates confidentiality obligations upon employment termination. This is an inconsistency because the original contract requires returning or destroying the information immediately and maintains confidentiality obligations even after termination of employment, which the changed contract fails to do. The scraped law snippet 1 refers to civil proceedings related to trade secret violations under 18 USC 1836, indicating the legal relevance of protecting trade secrets and the potential for legal action if they are misappropriated. By limiting the period for returning confidential information and terminating confidentiality obligations upon employment termination, the modified text opens the door for potential misappropriation, directly contradicting the legal intention to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: I agree to immediately report a ny known or susp ected violation of the confidentiality or security of PHI of patients of FAU to F AU’s Chief Compliance Officer.
perturbation.changed_text: I agree to report any known violation of the confidentiality or security of PHI of patients of FAU to FAU’s Chief Compliance Officer within 30 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 30 days of discovery' for reporting PHI violations creates a delay that conflicts with HIPAA's requirement for prompt reporting of breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA requires covered entities to report breaches of protected health information (PHI) without unreasonable delay, typically within 60 days of discovery and in some cases much sooner. The modified text, allowing a 30-day delay before reporting *known* violations, could violate HIPAA's mandate for prompt reporting, potentially hindering timely mitigation and notification efforts.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I agree to immediately report...'

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Guidance Materials | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: HIPAA Guidance Materials Small Providers, Small Health Plans, and other Small Businesses View materials about the Privacy Rule for small providers, small health plans and other small businesses. Covered Entities and Business Associates Joint OCR and FTC Publish Letters Sent to Hospital Systems and Telehealth Providers Warning about Privacy and Security Risks from Online Tracking Technologies * Bulletin on Online Tracking Technologies – This bulletin highlights the obligations of covered entities and business associates under the HIPAA Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules when using online tracking technologies. Guidance on HIPAA and Audio-Only Telehealth -This guidance explains how covered health care providers and health plans can use remote communication technologies to provide audio-only telehealth services when such communications are conducted in a manner that is consistent with the applicable requirements of the HIPAA Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules, including when OCR’s Notification of Enforcement Discretion for Telehealth is no longer in effect. Care Coordination and Continuity of Care – Frequently asked questions that clarify how the HIPAA Privacy Rule permits health plans to share protected health information (PHI) in a manner that furthers the HHS Secretary's goal of promoting coordinated care. Understanding Some of HIPAA's Permitted Uses and Disclosures - Topical fact sheets that provide examples of when PHI can be exchanged under HIPAA without first requiring a specific authorization from the patient, so long as other protections or conditions are met. Guidance on Significant Aspects of the Privacy Rule - A collection of documents explaining many provisions of the Privacy Rule including business associates, special topics such as disclosures for public health and research, and incidental uses and disclosures. Guidance on Individuals' Right to Access Health Information - This guidance explains the importance of providing individuals with the ability to access and obtain a copy of their health information. Guidance on HIPAA and Workplace Wellness Programs - This guidance explains the ways in which health information collected from or created about participants in a wellness program offered as part of a group health plan is protected by HIPAA. Guidance Regarding Methods for De-identification of Protected Health Information in Accordance with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Privacy Rule - This page provides guidance about methods and approaches to achieve de-identification in accordance with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA) Privacy Rule. The guidance explains and answers questions regarding the two methods that can be used to satisfy the Privacy Rule’s de-identification standard: Expert Determination and Safe Harbor. This guidance is intended to assist covered entities to understand what is de-identification, the general process by which de-identified information is created, and the options available for performing de-identification. Workshop on the HIPAA Privacy Rule's De-Identification Standard - Washington, DC - March 8th & 9th, 2010 Fast Facts for Covered Entities - Answers to many common questions and misconceptions about patient consent, incidental disclosures, child abuse reporting, electronic media, and other disclosures. Provider Guide: Communicating With a Patient's Family, Friends, or Other Persons Identified by the Patient - This is a guide for health care providers to help them determine when they can disclose a patient's health information to the pa
perturbation.justification: The change from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days of discovery' for reporting PHI violations directly contradicts the HIPAA mandate for prompt reporting. The Law Explanation states HIPAA requires reporting breaches 'without unreasonable delay'. While neither snippet explicitly defines 'unreasonable delay', the Law Explanation includes 'typically within 60 days of discovery and in some cases much sooner', thus allowing 30 days for reporting known violations could violate HIPAA's mandate for prompt reporting.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that violations of this Agreement may result in re vocation of my user privileges and/or disciplinary action, up to and including termination of my employment, affiliation or association with FAU, and that FAU may seek any civil or crimin al recourse and/or equitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that violations of this Agreement will automatically result in termination of my employment, affiliation or association with FAU, and that FAU may seek any civil or crimin al recourse and/or equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may result in' to 'will automatically result in' termination for any violation, no matter how minor, conflicts with employment laws requiring due process and consideration of mitigating circumstances before termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes and general employment law require a fair process before termination. Automatic termination, even for minor confidentiality breaches, could violate these requirements, especially if the employee is injured or disabled. For example, if an employee discloses confidential data while under the influence of prescribed medication following a workplace accident, automatic termination might violate Workers' Compensation laws prohibiting discrimination based on disability and requiring reasonable accommodation.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I understand that violations of this Agreement...'

perturbation.justification: The change from 'may result in' to 'will automatically result in' for any violation directly contradicts the due process rights inherent in employment law, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While the provided law snippets do not directly specify employment law or due process, the Law Explanation emphasizes that automatic termination, especially for minor breaches, could violate employment laws requiring fair process. The automatic termination clause removes the employer's discretion to consider mitigating circumstances or provide warnings, thus contradicting the standard legal expectation of due process in employment matters.

perturbation.original_text: Thi s information may include, but i s not limited to, patient health records, as well as information regarding human res ources, payroll, fiscal matters, research, and strategic pl anning, and may exist in any form, including electronic, video, spoken, or written.
perturbation.changed_text: Thi s information includes patient health records, as well as information regarding human res ources, payroll, fiscal matters, research, and strategic pl anning, and may exist in electronic form only.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may include, but is not limited to' to 'includes' and 'any form' to 'electronic form only' restricts the definition of confidential information in a way that conflicts with the broader protections required by HIPAA for all forms of PHI.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA's definition of Protected Health Information (PHI) encompasses any information, whether oral, written, or electronic, that relates to the past, present, or future physical or mental health or condition of an individual. By limiting the definition of confidential information to only electronic forms, the modified text excludes other forms of PHI (e.g., written notes, spoken communications), thus reducing the scope of protection below the HIPAA standard.  This violates the broad definition of PHI under HIPAA.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'FAU has a legal responsibility...'

perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts the definition of confidential information to 'electronic form only,' which directly contradicts HIPAA's broad definition of Protected Health Information (PHI) encompassing oral, written, and electronic forms. The law explanation asserts that this limitation reduces the scope of protection below the HIPAA standard, violating the broad definition of PHI. Snippet 1, although incomplete, indicates the existence of definitions within 45 CFR 160.103, where a comprehensive definition of PHI, including all forms, would be expected. The modified contract text, by excluding non-electronic forms of PHI, creates a legal contradiction as it fails to comply with HIPAA's standard.
